title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Value of Higher Education in the Digital Age

texts.A: The rising cost of a university degree is becoming increasingly difficult to justify in a world where the finest lectures from the most prestigious institutions are freely available online. Young people are accumulating decades of debt for an experience that is growing ever more impersonal and commodified. [40]We are clinging to a medieval model of 'the campus' that was designed for an era of information scarcity, when the professor was the sole gatekeeper of knowledge.[/40] That era has simply ended. [37]For most professions, a six-month intensive 'boot camp' followed by a structured apprenticeship would be far more effective than four years of largely disconnected academic theory.[/37] Employers themselves are beginning to recognize this shift, with many leading technology firms quietly dropping degree requirements from their hiring criteria. [39]The university has become a 'credential mill'—a mechanism for employers to filter candidates based on social class rather than actual demonstrated ability.[/39] This is fundamentally unjust and unsustainable.
texts.B: [38]University is about far more than mere 'job training'; it is the only dedicated period in a person's life set aside for the deliberate development of critical thinking and the genuine exploration of diverse, challenging ideas.[/38] [40]To reduce education entirely to its 'economic utility' is to profoundly miss the point of human flourishing.[/40] I agree that the student debt crisis is a serious and urgent issue that demands a comprehensive policy response, but the solution is increased public funding, not the dismantling of a centuries-old institution. A society that lacks a broad-based liberal arts education produces citizens who are dangerously vulnerable to demagoguery, simplistic sloganeering, and intellectual narrow-mindedness. The university is not simply a factory producing workers; it is a crucible for producing thoughtful, informed citizens capable of participating meaningfully in democratic life. We should be expanding access to this transformative experience, not abandoning it in favour of short-term vocational fixes.
texts.C: [39]I share the view that the rampant 'credentialism' of modern hiring practices is a genuine and deeply troubling problem, but I fundamentally disagree that freely available online courses constitute an adequate replacement for the full university experience.[/39] Learning is, at its core, an inherently social process that requires genuine human interaction to reach its fullest potential. [38]The informal networks forged over shared meals, the heated peer-to-peer debates in seminar rooms, and the long-term mentorship offered by a truly engaged professor cannot meaningfully be replicated by a pre-recorded video lecture consumed in isolation.[/38] The productive friction of a real intellectual community is itself a formative and irreplaceable part of the educational process. However, we cannot honestly pretend the current university system is adequate or equitable. We urgently need to reform the curriculum to reflect contemporary realities. A forward-looking 'hybrid' model should combine the intellectual depth of traditional academic inquiry with the practical, data-driven, and entrepreneurial skills clearly required by the rapidly evolving modern economy. Reform, not abandonment, is the only truly responsible and defensible path forward.
texts.D: The prevailing debate over 'theory versus practice' is, in my considered view, a significant and ultimately misleading distraction from the real and more important question at stake in higher education policy. The genuine and enduring value of a university degree lies in its powerful signal of 'grit'—the demonstrated capacity to complete a lengthy, demanding, and intellectually challenging project sustained over an extended period. While I broadly share the widespread skepticism regarding the current, often extortionate, price tag attached to higher education in many countries, [37]I firmly believe that the 'academic theory' so readily and dismissively criticized by reformers is precisely what enables workers to remain genuinely and lastingly adaptable across a constantly shifting career landscape.[/37] If you acquire only a narrow technical 'skill' in a short boot camp, you risk becoming professionally obsolete the very moment that particular technology is superseded or automated away. A deep theoretical grounding, by contrast, teaches you how to learn itself, building transferable mental frameworks.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the value of 'academic theory'?
questions.explanation: Expert A dismisses academic theory in favor of 'boot camps.' Expert D argues that academic theory is exactly what makes workers 'adaptable' and helps them 'learn how to learn.'

questions.question: Which expert shares C's opinion about the social dimension of learning?
questions.explanation: Expert C says learning is a 'social process' and mentions 'peer-to-peer debates.' Expert B also emphasizes the 'exploration of diverse ideas' and critical thinking that happens within the university community.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to A regarding the 'credential' function of degrees?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls university a 'credential mill.' Expert C shares the view that 'credentialism' in modern hiring is a problem.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the primary purpose of a university education?
questions.explanation: Expert B believes the purpose is 'human flourishing' and 'critical thinking.' Expert A sees it primarily as an outdated and inefficient model of preparation for work that should be replaced by practical training.
